analysis: The stronger heuristics share a clear structure: Pareto prefiltering, a fast rect-volume (single-point) surrogate to rank and shortlist candidates, a stamped lazy‑greedy selection that only recomputes expensive marginal hypervolume for heap tops, aggressive caching of HV evaluations, and a bounded local single‑swap refinement. They balance cost/quality by shrinking the evaluation universe (shortlists, nondominated filtering, top‑outsiders) and reusing expensive work (shared MC samples, hv_cache). Weaker variants lean heavily on full Monte‑Carlo (large M), expensive pairwise loops, or repeated allocation (rebuilding arrays/frozensets) inside selection loops, causing memory and Python‑level overhead. Common efficiency opportunities: reuse a single MC sample and dominated mask across iterations; incremental updates to covered samples instead of recomputing; prefer sorted-tuple keys or integer bitmaps over frozensets for cache keys; avoid O(N^2) Python loops for nondomination by vectorized/blocked checks; adapt shortlist size to k and D; seed heaps with cheap surrogates (rect, sample‑counts) and use stamps to amortize exact HV calls. Careful parameterization of MC M, pool_size, and swap budgets (dependent on k and D) gives most of the runtime/quality tradeoff.
experience: Prefer stamped lazy‑greedy seeded by cheap surrogates, reuse one shared MC sample and dominated mask, cache HVs with lightweight keys, vectorize nondomination, adapt shortlist and M to k and D, and limit local search to top outsiders. These yield large time savings with little HV loss.
current_reflection: Prefer stamped lazy‑greedy seeded by cheap surrogates, reuse one shared MC sample and dominated mask, cache HVs with lightweight keys, vectorize nondomination, adapt shortlist and M to k and D, and limit local search to top outsiders. These yield large time savings with little HV loss.
good_reflections: Always prefilter (non‑dominated), compute cheap single‑point estimates, drive a lazy max‑heap of marginal gains with stamps, recompute exact marginal HV only on heap top, then apply a small bounded local swap. Cache results, avoid rebuilding arrays/objects in loops, vectorize dominated checks, and cap stochastic search effort. This combo maximizes HV per evaluation at low runtime.

Prefer Pareto prefilter + stamped lazy greedy with single‑rect warmstarts and an adaptive shortlist (c·k). Cache HVs keyed cheaply (sorted tuple or bitmask), compute marginal = hv(S∪{p})−hv(S) only for heap tops, use pygmo when available, fallback to adaptive Monte‑Carlo with bitset chunking, and limit swap trials. Vectorize nondomination and avoid repeated frozenset/array conversions.

Keep the stamped lazy-greedy backbone: Pareto prefilter → adaptive rect‑surrogate shortlist → stamped heap + HV-cache → tiny bounded swap. Map original indices into a compact pool, compute HVs with pygmo when available, otherwise reuse a small shared MC sample for marginal estimates. Limit pairwise/diversity costs and cap shortlist/swap budgets adaptively by k to maximize HV per compute.

Seed a stamped lazy-greedy heap with normalized singleton rect scores, keep a compact shortlist ∝ k, cache HVs using canonical sorted tuples or bitmasks, and recompute exact marginal only for popped items. Reuse Monte‑Carlo dominated masks (bitsets) to compute marginals incrementally; adapt sample size to D and pool size. Limit and prioritize swap trials. Profile hotspots and avoid O(N^2) Python loops—vectorize or chunk.

Prefilter by Pareto, build an adaptive shortlist using a blended surrogate (rect volume + normalized shared global MC dominated counts + a lightweight diversity term), then run a stamped lazy greedy heap that recomputes true gains only at the top (use pgm exact HV when available). Cache bitset dominated masks and HV results, use a tiny focused swap pool, vectorize nondomination, and adapt sample/pool sizes to N, D, k for speed and reliable marginal estimates.
bad_reflections: Favor a lazy‑greedy core: Pareto prefilter → top‑c·k shortlist by rect volumes → stamped heap with cached marginals and only top‑of‑heap exact HV recompute → bounded local swaps. Reuse arrays, vectorize dominance checks, cap Monte‑Carlo work, and call a C HV library (pygmo) or incremental HV routines to cut Python overhead.

Prefer Pareto prefilter + rect surrogate shortlist + stamped lazy heap, but enforce: (1) cached HVs keyed by frozenset(indices), (2) reuse a single pygmo.hypervolume object per distinct point set when possible, (3) vectorize dominance and sample precomputation, (4) adapt shortlist size to k and N, and (5) bound local swaps — this yields high HV with predictable runtime.

Always Pareto‑filter first, then shortlist by single‑point rect volumes plus a lightweight diversity pick. Use a stamped lazy greedy heap with exact HV from a fast C library; cache marginal gains keyed by compact bitmasks. Limit Monte‑Carlo to large‑D fallback with small M. Cap swap neighborhood and parallelize exact evaluations. Vectorize dominance checks and avoid hot Python loops or frequent frozenset creation.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: stamped lazy‑greedy, Pareto prefilter, surrogate shortlist, cached HV, bitset dominated masks, shared MC sample, vectorized nondomination, adaptive M/shortlist, bounded swaps.
- Advice: Pareto‑filter, build an adaptive rect/surrogate shortlist (∝ k), seed a stamped lazy heap, recompute exact marginal HV only for popped items, reuse one MC sample and bitset masks, cache HVs with cheap keys, vectorize nondomination, set M and shortlist size by k and D, limit swaps to top outsiders.
- Avoid: rebuilding arrays/objects in loops, frozenset/slow keys, full pairwise recomputes, unbounded MC/swap budgets, O(N^2) Python loops.
- Explanation: These measures cut runtime sharply with minimal hypervolume loss and gracefully fall back to CHV (pygmo) or adaptive MC when needed.
